name: Läubli, Thomas
recent_1: Wagenblast, F., Läubli, T., Seibt, R., Luger, T., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2026). Bilateral trapezius activity during a dual task depends on age and sex – Results of an experimental laboratory study. Journal of Electromyography and Kinesiology, 88, 103129. DOI: 10.1016/j.jelekin.2026.103129
recent_1_synopsis: This study examined bilateral upper trapezius EMG activity in 48 participants (younger/older, male/female) across four dual-task conditions combining two wrist muscle load levels with easy or difficult cognitive memory loads. Results showed that trapezius activity and relative rest time depended on age and sex in interaction with the muscular and cognitive load, extending understanding of how demographic factors modulate secondary neck-shoulder muscle recruitment during repetitive arm work with cognitive demands.
recent_2: Wagenblast, F., Läubli, T., Seibt, R., Rieger, M. A., & Steinhilber, B. (2023). Wrist Extensor Muscle Fatigue During a Dual Task With Two Muscular and Cognitive Load Levels in Younger and Older Adults. Human Factors, 65(8), 1594–1608. DOI: 10.1177/00187208231218196
recent_2_synopsis: Participants performed a dual task combining intermittent isometric wrist contractions at two load levels with a concurrent cognitive memory task, with EMG measures of wrist extensor fatigue recorded across sessions. The study revealed how cognitive load interacts with muscular load to modulate fatigue development, and how these effects differ between younger and older adults, with implications for workplace design in populations with aging workforces.
recent_3: Kim, D., Nicoletti, C., Soedirdjo, S. D. H., Baghi, R., Garcia, M.-G., Läubli, T., Wild, P., Botter, A., & Martin, B. J. (2021). Effect of Periodic Voluntary Interventions on Trapezius Activation and Fatigue During Light Upper Limb Activity. Human Factors, 63(8), 1332–1345. DOI: 10.1177/00187208211050723
recent_3_synopsis: This laboratory study examined whether periodic voluntary muscle contractions during light, sustained upper limb work could modify trapezius activation patterns and reduce local muscle fatigue. Findings indicated that deliberate brief interventions altered motor unit recruitment and reduced fatigue indicators, suggesting that active self-initiated breaks targeting the trapezius may be a practical strategy to mitigate work-related neck-shoulder disorders.
seminal_1: Hünting, W., Läubli, T., & Grandjean, E. (1981). Postural and visual loads at VDT workplaces I. Constrained postures. Ergonomics, 24(12), 917–931. DOI: 10.1080/00140138108924914
seminal_1_synopsis: One of the earliest systematic studies of posture and visual strain among visual display terminal (VDT) workers, this paper documented how data-entry and conversational terminal work imposed constrained postures and head positions that differed markedly from conventional office work. With approximately 204 Crossref citations (likely far more via Google Scholar), it became a foundational reference establishing that VDT work created distinct ergonomic risk profiles, directly informing subsequent decades of standards development and workplace intervention research.
seminal_2: Garcia, M.-G., Läubli, T., & Martin, B. J. (2015). Long-Term Muscle Fatigue After Standing Work. Human Factors, 57(7), 1162–1173. DOI: 10.1177/0018720815590293
seminal_2_synopsis: Using muscle twitch force as an objective fatigue indicator, this study demonstrated that five hours of simulated standing work produced lower-limb fatigue that persisted beyond 30 minutes after the workday ended, with no meaningful difference between younger and older adults. With approximately 59 Crossref citations, the paper reframed standing work from a putative health benefit to a musculoskeletal risk with sustained physiological consequences, becoming a key empirical anchor in debates over sit-stand workstation recommendations.
expertise_overview: Thomas Läubli is a Swiss occupational health researcher whose work centers on the physiological mechanisms by which sustained and repetitive work causes musculoskeletal disorders, particularly in the neck-shoulder region and lower limbs. His primary methodological tools include surface electromyography (EMG) — including motor unit analysis and spectral fatigue indices — and muscle twitch force assessment to quantify acute and long-term muscle fatigue under ecologically valid work conditions. He has studied how task characteristics such as muscular load level, cognitive demand, work-rest schedules, and posture (prolonged standing, seated VDT work) interact to drive fatigue and disorder risk. A recurring theme is how age and sex moderate these physiological responses, with implications for ergonomic accommodation of diverse workforces. His applied research addresses interventions (work rotations, active breaks, workstation redesign) and occupational populations including office workers, nurses, and industrial workers. Institutionally he has been affiliated with ETH Zürich and the University of Zürich, and he has contributed to the scientific community as chair of the ICOH Scientific Committee on Musculoskeletal Disorders.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. The four pre-verified Human Factors DOIs uniquely locate the correct Thomas Läubli (occupational health/ergonomics, Swiss, ETH/UZH), distinguished from a philosopher also named Thomas Läubli at UZH Philosophisches Seminar. Identity confirmed via PubMed (61 publications), ResearchGate profile (University of Tübingen affiliation for recent collaborations), and web sources describing his ETH Zürich affiliation and ICOH SC Musculoskeletal Disorders chair role. DOI verification via Crossref API (curl, Crossref works endpoint, June 2026): 10.1016/j.jelekin.2026.103129 — VERIFIED (HTTP 200, title matches); 10.1177/00187208231218196 — VERIFIED (HTTP 200, title matches); 10.1177/00187208211050723 — VERIFIED (HTTP 200, title matches); 10.1080/00140138108924914 — VERIFIED (HTTP 200, title matches, 204 Crossref citations); 10.1177/0018720815590293 — VERIFIED (HTTP 200, title matches, 59 Crossref citations). Citation counts sourced from Crossref is-referenced-by-count field, retrieved June 2026 — these undercount true citations relative to Google Scholar. The 1981 Hünting/Läubli/Grandjean paper author field in Crossref is malformed (Grandjean listed under 'given' of second author entry), but title, journal, volume, pages, and DOI are unambiguous. No DOI-less older papers were cited.
